Rest, GraphQL or BFFThe choice isn’t ideological.
REST and GraphQL solve different problems — and the Backend for Frontend (BFF) pattern is often the real answer to a problem that’s wrongly blamed on the choice between the two.
The question isn’t:
“Should we use REST or GraphQL?”
The better question is:
“What problem are we actually trying to solve?”
Let’s look at the practical differences.
The concrete problem REST handles poorly: over-fetching and under-fetching
REST is excellent for many use cases, especially simple CRUD operations.
But some frontend applications eventually run into two familiar problems: over-fetching and under-fetching.
Imagine a coupon detail screen.
You request:
GET /api/coupons/42
The API returns the entire coupon object, including fields this particular screen doesn’t need.
That’s over-fetching.
Now imagine the screen also needs related products and recent usage history.
You might end up making several requests:
GET /api/coupons/42
GET /api/coupons/42/related-products
GET /api/coupons/42/usage-history
One screen.
Three network calls.
That’s under-fetching: the initial resource doesn’t contain enough information to render the screen, so the frontend has to orchestrate additional requests.
GraphQL approaches the problem differently.
Instead of requesting a predefined resource representation, the client explicitly declares the data it needs:
query GetDiscountDetail($id: ID!) {
coupon(id: $id) {
code
discountRate
relatedProducts {
id
name
}
usageHistory(limit: 5) {
date
customerId
}
}
}The query describes exactly what this screen needs.
No unnecessary fields.
No separate requests for related data.
At least, that’s the promise.
And that’s an important distinction: GraphQL solves the data-fetching problem at the API contract level. But it doesn’t automatically solve the performance problem underneath. We’ll come back to that.
Consuming GraphQL in Angular
From an Angular application, a GraphQL client such as Apollo makes this interaction relatively straightforward.
For example:
const GET_DISCOUNT_DETAIL = gql`
query GetDiscountDetail($id: ID!) {
coupon(id: $id) {
id
code
discountRate
relatedProducts {
id
name
}
}
}
`;@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class DiscountGraphQLService {
private readonly apollo = inject(Apollo); getDiscountDetail(id: string) {
return this.apollo
.watchQuery<{ coupon: CouponDetail }>({
query: GET_DISCOUNT_DETAIL,
variables: { id }
})
.valueChanges
.pipe(
map(({ data }) => data.coupon)
);
}
}The frontend asks for the exact shape of data it needs.
This becomes particularly interesting when different screens consume the same underlying domain.
A list might need:
id
code
discountRateA detail page might need:
id
code
discountRate
relatedProducts
usageHistory
customerA mobile screen might need only:
id
code
discountRateWith GraphQL, the client can select the fields it needs from the same schema.
But there’s another architectural pattern that addresses this problem from a different angle.
The Backend for Frontend.
The BFF pattern: a dedicated aggregation layer per frontend
Imagine a company with:

• an Angular web application;

• a mobile application;

• a billing service;

• a catalog service;

• a user service.
The web application needs one combination of data. The mobile application needs another. Instead of forcing both frontends to consume exactly the same backend contract, each frontend gets its own dedicated backend layer.
Conceptually:
┌─────────────────┐
│ Angular Web │
└────────┬────────┘
│
▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Web BFF │
└───────┬─────────┘
│
┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
▼ ▼ ▼
Billing API Catalog API User API
┌─────────────────┐
│ Mobile App │
└────────┬────────┘
│
▼
┌─────────────────┐
│ Mobile BFF │
└───────┬─────────┘
│
┌───────┴───────┐
▼ ▼
Billing API Catalog APIA BFF is neither REST nor GraphQL by nature.
It’s a backend layer dedicated to one specific frontend.It can aggregate several internal services and shape the response around what that frontend actually needs.
This matters because mobile and web often have fundamentally different requirements.
A mobile application might need:

• fewer fields;

• smaller payloads;

• fewer network round trips;

• responses optimized for unreliable connections.
A web application might need:

• richer data;

• more relationships;

• larger datasets;

• different aggregation rules.
One generic API contract doesn’t always serve both particularly well.
The BFF pattern lets each frontend have a backend tailored to its needs.
And here’s the important part:
The BFF doesn’t care whether the internal communication uses REST or GraphQL.
You can have:
Frontend
↓
REST BFF
↓
Internal REST APIsOr:
Frontend
↓
GraphQL BFF
↓
Internal REST APIsOr:
Frontend
↓
GraphQL BFF
↓
Internal GraphQL APIsThe BFF and the API protocol solve different architectural problems.
The real deciding criterion: the shape of the need, not the tech trend
There is no universal winner.
A useful rule of thumb is:
Choose REST when:

• you’re exposing simple CRUD operations;

• resources map naturally to URLs;

• the data structure is relatively stable;

• the backend team already has strong REST expertise;

• simplicity and operational maturity are priorities.
Choose GraphQL when:

• screens have highly variable data requirements;

• clients need deeply nested relationships;

• multiple clients consume the same domain differently;

• reducing frontend orchestration is important;

• the API is primarily designed around client-driven data requirements.
Consider a BFF when:

• you have several frontends;

• web and mobile have significantly different requirements;

• the frontend is currently orchestrating calls to multiple backend services;

• you want to aggregate internal APIs without exposing that complexity to clients.
And these aren’t mutually exclusive choices.
You can absolutely have:
REST + BFF
or:
GraphQL + BFF
The BFF is an architectural pattern.
REST and GraphQL are API approaches.
They operate at different levels of the architecture.
The classic performance trap: the N+1 problem
GraphQL solves one problem particularly well:
The client can ask for exactly the data it needs.
But there’s a trap.
Consider this query:
query {
coupons {
id
relatedProducts {
name
}
}
}At first glance, this looks efficient.
One request.
One query.
But what happens inside the server?
A naive resolver implementation might do this:
Query all coupons
│
├── Coupon 1 → Query products
├── Coupon 2 → Query products
├── Coupon 3 → Query products
├── Coupon 4 → Query products
└── ...If you return 100 coupons, you might trigger 100 additional database queries.
That’s the N+1 problem. One query to fetch the coupons. Then one additional query for every coupon.
In production, this can become a serious performance problem. The solution is typically batching and per-request caching.
One common approach is DataLoader:
const productLoader = new DataLoader(
async (couponIds: readonly string[]) => {
const products =
await productService.findByCouponIds([...couponIds]);
return couponIds.map((id) =>
products.filter((p) => p.couponId === id)
);
}
);Instead of executing one database query per coupon, the resolver collects the requested IDs and retrieves the related data in batches.
Conceptually:
100 coupons
│
▼
DataLoader
│
▼
1 batched query
│
▼
Products grouped by couponThis is one of the reasons GraphQL isn’t simply:
“REST, but better.”
GraphQL gives you more flexibility at the API boundary. But that flexibility comes with additional implementation responsibilities. Resolvers need to be designed carefully.
N+1 queries need to be controlled.
Authorization needs to be considered at field and resolver level.
Query complexity and depth may need to be limited.
Caching can also be more complicated than traditional HTTP caching.
GraphQL is powerful.
But it isn’t free.
Introducing a BFF/GraphQL layer progressively
Here’s another advantage of the BFF approach. You don’t necessarily need to rewrite your entire backend.
Imagine you already have a legacy REST API. You could introduce a GraphQL BFF in front of it:
┌───────────────┐
│ Frontend │
└───────┬───────┘
│
▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ GraphQL BFF │
│ │
│ Resolvers │
└──────────┬──────────┘
│
▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Existing REST API │
│ unchanged │
└─────────────────────┘A GraphQL resolver can call an existing REST API internally.
The frontend gets a new GraphQL contract without requiring the underlying backend to be rewritten immediately.
This makes the BFF a useful facade layer.
You can introduce it progressively.
One feature at a time.
One domain at a time.
One service at a time.
The existing system continues to run while the new layer gradually takes responsibility for new frontend requirements.
This is closely related to the Strangler Fig pattern:
Don’t rewrite everything at once.
Instead, introduce the new architecture alongside the old one and progressively move responsibilities to the new layer. This is often a much more realistic migration strategy than a “big bang” rewrite.
Evolving a GraphQL schema without breaking consumers
GraphQL also offers an interesting approach to API evolution.
Suppose you have:
type Coupon {
discountRate: Float
}You eventually decide that discountPercentage is a clearer name. Instead of immediately removing the old field, you can deprecate it:
type Coupon {
discountRate: Float
@deprecated(
reason: "Use discountPercentage instead"
)
discountPercentage: Float
}The old field remains available while consumers migrate. This allows different clients to move at their own pace.
Compared with traditional REST API versioning, where teams often introduce a new URL such as:
/api/v1/coupons
/api/v2/couponsGraphQL generally evolves the schema over time rather than creating a completely separate URL version for every breaking change.
But there is an important nuance. GraphQL doesn’t magically eliminate breaking changes. If you remove a field that a client still relies on, you can still break that client.
The advantage is that schema evolution can happen more granularly, field by field, rather than requiring an entirely new API version for every evolution.
The key is discipline:
Deprecate first. Migrate consumers. Remove later.
So, REST or GraphQL?
The answer is still:
It depends.
REST remains an excellent default for direct CRUD and resource-oriented APIs. GraphQL earns its keep when clients have highly variable, deeply nested data requirements and need more control over the shape of the response.
The BFF pattern addresses another problem entirely:
“We have several frontends with different needs, and our backend doesn’t fit all of them.”
And that’s why I don’t think the REST vs GraphQL debate is particularly useful when framed as a battle.
The real question is:
What problem are you trying to solve?
If the problem is simple resource access, REST might be all you need.
If the problem is highly variable data requirements, GraphQL may be a better fit.
If the problem is that your web and mobile applications need fundamentally different backend contracts, a BFF might be the architectural answer.
And sometimes, the best architecture is:
REST internally + GraphQL BFF externally.
Or:
REST internally + REST BFF externally.
The protocol is a tool.
The architecture should follow the problem.
Don’t choose GraphQL because it’s trendy.
Don’t choose REST because it’s familiar.
And don’t introduce a BFF just because everyone else has one.
Start with the problem.
Then choose the simplest architecture that actually solves it.
